subject: geography
title: KS3 Geography — Glaciation 2
subtitle: Reading passages on depositional landforms and glacial landscape management
description: Short reading passages covering how glaciers deposit material, how landforms such as moraines and drumlins form, and how glacial landscapes are managed for tourism. The passages also explain conflict between visitors, local people and conservation aims.

data.sourcePassage: When a glacier is moving, it carries rock fragments and sediment. If the glacier slows down or melts, it loses energy and drops this material. The deposited material is called moraine. Different patterns of moraine form in different places. Terminal moraine marks the glacier's furthest advance. Lateral moraine forms along the sides of the glacier. If two glaciers join, their side deposits can merge into a line of medial moraine. Ground moraine is spread across the valley floor after the ice has retreated.
data.targetPassage: When a glacier is moving, it carries rock fragments and sediment. If the glacier slows down or melts, it loses energy and drops this material. The deposited material is called moraine. Different patterns of moraine form in different places. Terminal moraine marks the glacier's furthest advance. Lateral moraine forms along the sides of the glacier. If two glaciers join, their side deposits can merge into a line of medial moraine. Ground moraine is spread across the valley floor after the ice has retreated.

questions.question: What is the name for material dropped by a glacier?
Which type of moraine shows the glacier's furthest advance?
Why might medial moraine only form in some valleys?
questions.modelAnswer: It only forms where two glaciers meet and their lateral moraines join together.

questions.question: Why is terminal moraine useful to geographers studying a glacier?
questions.modelAnswer: It shows how far the glacier advanced, so it helps geographers identify the former limit of the ice.

data.sourcePassage: Drumlins are depositional landforms made mainly of till. They are shaped like half-buried eggs or teardrops and are usually found in groups called drumlin fields. One explanation is that a glacier deposits material when it meets an obstruction or reaches a flatter valley floor. As the glacier keeps moving, the deposited material becomes streamlined into a smooth, tapered hill. Glaciers also leave erratics. These are large rocks carried away from their original source and dropped in an area with different rock types.
data.targetPassage: Drumlins are depositional landforms made mainly of till. They are shaped like half-buried eggs or teardrops and are usually found in groups called drumlin fields. One explanation is that a glacier deposits material when it meets an obstruction or reaches a flatter valley floor. As the glacier keeps moving, the deposited material becomes streamlined into a smooth, tapered hill. Glaciers also leave erratics. These are large rocks carried away from their original source and dropped in an area with different rock types.

questions.question: What shape are drumlins usually described as?
questions.modelAnswer: They are usually shaped like teardrops or half-buried eggs.

questions.question: What is a group of drumlins called?
How does continued glacier movement help shape a drumlin?
questions.modelAnswer: As the glacier continues moving, it streamlines the deposited sediment into a smooth tapered hill.

questions.question: Why can erratics give clues about past glacier movement?
questions.modelAnswer: They show that rocks were transported from a different place by moving ice before being dropped.

data.sourcePassage: Glacial landscapes are often spectacular and attract many tourists. This can help local economies because visitors spend money in shops, cafes and accommodation. Some people may also move into glacial areas after retirement. However, these landscapes are fragile. If tourism is not managed carefully, paths can become badly eroded, vegetation can be damaged and traffic can overwhelm small settlements. Because of these pressures, environmentalists, conservationists and local people all have an interest in how tourism is managed.
data.targetPassage: Glacial landscapes are often spectacular and attract many tourists. This can help local economies because visitors spend money in shops, cafes and accommodation. Some people may also move into glacial areas after retirement. However, these landscapes are fragile. If tourism is not managed carefully, paths can become badly eroded, vegetation can be damaged and traffic can overwhelm small settlements. Because of these pressures, environmentalists, conservationists and local people all have an interest in how tourism is managed.

questions.question: Give one reason why glacial landscapes attract tourists.
questions.modelAnswer: They attract tourists because they are dramatic and beautiful landscapes.

questions.question: Which of these is a benefit of tourism mentioned in the passage?
options: It brings money to local businesses
It removes the need for management
questions.modelAnswer: It brings money to local businesses

questions.question: Why do glacial landscapes need careful management even when tourism brings benefits?
questions.modelAnswer: They need careful management because they are fragile and can be damaged by erosion, vegetation loss and traffic pressure.

questions.question: Why might local people and conservationists disagree about tourism in the same area?
questions.modelAnswer: Local people may value jobs and income from tourism, while conservationists may be more concerned about protecting the landscape from damage.

data.sourcePassage: Different tourist activities can create different problems in glacial landscapes. Overused footpaths can destroy vegetation and erode the ground. Speedboats can create noise and water pollution. Dogs may disturb cattle on farmland, and heavy traffic can lead to congestion, air pollution and accidents. Managers try to reduce these conflicts in practical ways. They may divert popular routes to let land recover, set speed limits on lakes, keep dogs on leads around farms, promote public transport and bike paths, and educate visitors through visitor centres.
data.targetPassage: Different tourist activities can create different problems in glacial landscapes. Overused footpaths can destroy vegetation and erode the ground. Speedboats can create noise and water pollution. Dogs may disturb cattle on farmland, and heavy traffic can lead to congestion, air pollution and accidents. Managers try to reduce these conflicts in practical ways. They may divert popular routes to let land recover, set speed limits on lakes, keep dogs on leads around farms, promote public transport and bike paths, and educate visitors through visitor centres.

questions.question: What problem can speedboats cause?
questions.modelAnswer: Speedboats can cause noise and water pollution.

questions.question: Which solution is used to reduce damage on overused paths?
Why might keeping dogs on leads help farmers?
questions.modelAnswer: It reduces the chance that dogs will disturb cattle or livestock on farmland.

questions.question: Which management method in the passage is most focused on changing visitor behaviour rather than controlling vehicles or routes?
questions.modelAnswer: Educating visitors through visitor centres is most focused on changing behaviour.
